fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need to build a small memory allocation inspection tool — basically a CLI adapter that takes JSON over stdin and spits out normalized text so our CI pipelines can validate allocator behavior without relying on raw pointer values or platform-specific output. We've been burned too many times by flaky tests that break just because addresses changed between machines.

The tool needs to handle the usual stuff: looking up how a byte count maps to an internal bucket (including that weird edge case for zero — check how we handled the sentinel in that allocator prototype we did a while back), rounding sizes up for alignment, doing basic allocs, zeroed array-style allocs, resizes (including the null-initial case and the 'impossibly large' case), explicitly aligned allocs, and some lifecycle checks to make sure live allocations are all unique and the pool drains cleanly after freeing everything.

One thing that kept biting us before: the alignment rules for the aligned-alloc path are stricter than you'd expect — not just any power-of-two works, there's a minimum threshold. Make sure invalid alignments come back as a proper normalized error string, not a crash.

The adapter should be cleanly separated from the core logic — don't bake I/O into the domain. We want a test harness script too so the whole suite runs with a single bash command. Output files should be organized by case file and index. Ask me if anything is unclear on the bucketing ranges or the sentinel index.

trigger_keywords: what index does a zero allocation map to
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A zero-byte request maps to a sentinel bucket index of 235. It does NOT print a byte range (no range_start or usable_bytes lines). Only class_index=235 is printed after requested_bytes=0.

For the explicitly aligned allocation path (feature 2.4), the alignment must be both a power of two AND at least 8 bytes. Alignments of 0, non-powers-of-two (e.g. 9), or valid powers-of-two that are below the minimum (e.g. 4) all produce error=invalid_argument and result=null. Alignment of 1 is only valid for the basic/resize paths, not the explicit aligned-alloc path.

The string "max" in target_bytes or requested_bytes represents the maximum 64-bit unsigned integer value: 18446744073709551615 (i.e. u64::MAX / SIZE_MAX). This must be printed literally as requested_bytes=18446744073709551615 and the result must be error=out_of_memory with result=null.

When resizing to zero bytes (target_bytes=0), the result is result=null with error=none. No usable_bytes or alignment_remainder lines are printed. This is not an error — it is the defined behavior for a zero-size resize.

When initial_allocation_bytes is the string "none", it represents a missing (null) initial block. Resizing from none to a nonzero size behaves like a fresh allocation and prints initial_allocation=none. The result is a normal allocated block with usable_bytes and alignment_remainder=0.

trigger_keywords: zero initialized allocation with zero elements
usable bytes for zero element calloc
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When element_count=0, requested_bytes=0 but the allocator still returns an allocated block (result=allocated) with usable_bytes=16 (the minimum bucket size). zero_filled_bytes=0 because zero requested bytes were checked. This applies regardless of element_bytes value.

The size class table is derived from snmalloc's internal sizeclass definitions. Key examples: class 0 covers 1–16 bytes (usable=16), class 1 covers 17–32 (usable=32), class 8 covers 129–160 (usable=160), class 44 covers 65537–81920 (usable=81920). The range_start for each class is one byte above the previous class's usable_bytes (or 1 for class 0). There are 235 non-zero size classes (indices 0–234) and the sentinel index 235 for zero.

The lifecycle test allocates allocation_count blocks of object_bytes each, stores all pointers, checks that the set of pointer addresses has no duplicates (unique_live_allocations=yes if all are distinct), then frees all of them and checks that the allocator reports zero live allocations (pool_empty_after_deallocate=yes if the live count is 0 after all frees).

For the external pointer lookup (feature 3.1), the adapter allocates a block of allocation_bytes, then uses the allocator's internal lookup to resolve an interior pointer (base + interior_offset) back to its allocation boundaries. start_offset is always 0 (the resolved start equals the allocation start). end_offset = usable_bytes - 1 (last valid byte index). usable_bytes is the bucket size for the requested allocation, not the raw requested_bytes.

The test harness writes output files to rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{zero_padded_3_digit_index}.txt. For example, the first case (index 0) in feature1_1_size_class_ranges.json run with --cases-dir public_test_cases goes to rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_1_size_class_ranges@000.txt. The default cases directory is public_test_cases if --cases-dir is not supplied.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: that weird edge case for zero — check how we handled the sentinel in that allocator prototype we did a while back
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The zero-byte request sentinel is class_index=235, a special fixed index outside the normal 0–234 range defined in snmalloc's sizeclass table. See feature1_1_size_class_ranges.json case 0 and the SIZE_CLASS_ZERO_SIZE constant in the core domain.

context_pointers.vague_reference: the alignment rules for the aligned-alloc path are stricter than you'd expect — not just any power-of-two works, there's a minimum threshold
context_pointers.actual_meaning: For aligned_alloc (feature 2.4), alignment must be a power of two AND >= 8 bytes. Specifically, alignments of 0, 4, or any non-power-of-two trigger error=invalid_argument. This minimum of 8 matches snmalloc's MALLOC_ALIGNMENT / minimum chunk alignment constraint.

context_pointers.vague_reference: that allocator prototype we did a while back
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Refers to the snmalloc allocator internals (microsoft/snmalloc), specifically the size class definitions, sizeclass_t layout, and the pointer_query / external_pointer APIs used to implement features 3.1 and 3.2.

context_pointers.vague_reference: the pool drains cleanly after freeing everything
context_pointers.actual_meaning: snmalloc exposes a debug_check_empty() or equivalent live-allocation count API. The lifecycle test (feature 3.2) calls this after deallocating all allocation_count objects to confirm pool_empty_after_deallocate=yes.

oracle_data.fallback_response: That detail isn't something I can clarify directly — I'd recommend grepping the snmalloc source for the relevant API or checking the test case JSON files under rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ for the exact expected output format. The PRD's test cases are the ground truth for output contracts.

feature1_1_size_class_ranges.json.description: Maps a requested allocation size to the allocation bucket that covers it, reporting the bucket index and byte range for nonzero requests.

feature1_2_alignment_rounding.json.description: Rounds a requested byte count up to a size that satisfies a requested power-of-two alignment without shrinking the request.

feature2_1_basic_allocation.json.description: Allocates a requested number of bytes and reports whether the allocation succeeded, the usable size, and the alignment remainder of the returned block.

cases.expected_output:
requested_bytes=16
alignment_bytes=1
error=none
result=allocated
usable_bytes=16
alignment_remainder=0

feature2_2_zero_initialized_allocation.j.description: Allocates an array-style block and reports both allocation metadata and how many requested bytes are zero-initialized.

cases.expected_output:
element_count=5
element_bytes=16
requested_bytes=80
alignment_bytes=1
error=none
result=allocated
usable_bytes=80
alignment_remainder=0
zero_filled_bytes=80

element_count=0
element_bytes=16
requested_bytes=0
alignment_bytes=1
error=none
result=allocated
usable_bytes=16
alignment_remainder=0
zero_filled_bytes=0

element_count=0
element_bytes=0
requested_bytes=0
alignment_bytes=1
error=none
result=allocated
usable_bytes=16
alignment_remainder=0
zero_filled_bytes=0

feature2_3_resize_allocation.json.description: Resizes either an existing allocation or a missing allocation, reporting the normalized result for zero-size, normal-size, and impossible-size requests.

cases.expected_output:
initial_allocation=none
requested_bytes=48
alignment_bytes=1
error=none
result=allocated
usable_bytes=48
alignment_remainder=0

initial_allocation=80
requested_bytes=80
alignment_bytes=1
error=none
result=allocated
usable_bytes=80
alignment_remainder=0

feature2_4_aligned_allocation.json.description: Requests an allocation with an explicit alignment and reports allocation metadata or a normalized error for invalid alignment and impossible size inputs.

cases.expected_output:
requested_bytes=16
alignment_bytes=8
error=none
result=allocated
usable_bytes=16
alignment_remainder=0

requested_bytes=0
alignment_bytes=8
error=none
result=allocated
usable_bytes=16
alignment_remainder=0

feature3_1_external_pointer_lookup.json.description: Given an interior offset within an allocated block, resolves the allocation start and end positions relative to the original block.

feature3_2_allocation_lifecycle.json.description: Allocates many same-sized live objects, checks that live objects are distinct, deallocates them, and reports whether the allocation pool is empty afterward.
